replies.content: The three spheres in the zone of the Absolute Now are the three divine levels above the realms of manifest existence and time: the sphere of the Absolute, the sphere of the Supreme Being of the universe, and the sphere of the Solar Logos. Below that is the world of manifestation, in which involution and evolution cycle through a sequence of seven stages, traditionally called the Saturn, Sun, Moon, Earth, Jupiter, Venus, and Vulcan periods. Today's humanity is going through its Earth period right now.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: By “involution”, what is meant that is different in meaning from “evolution”?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Involution is the process of descent into manifestation; evolution is the process of rising back up out of manifestation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Vulcan?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yeah, there's that. In the mid-19th century reputable astronomers believed there was a planet between Mercury and the Sun, due to wobbles in the orbit of Mercury that didn't make sense any other way. It was named Vulcan, and some astronomers claimed to have seen it, although others looked for it and didn't see it. In 1915 Einstein showed that his theory of relativity explained Mercury's orbital wobble perfectly, and Vulcan evaporated like the phantom it was. In the meantime, however, it had been picked up by astrologers and occultists, and the particular current of Rosicrucian mysticism from which this diagram comes adopted it as a symbol -- think "closest to the Sun." So the terminology still lingers, though the teaching documents point out that this is purely symbolic and doesn't have to do with the actual (or imaginary) planets involved.

comments.title: CGD-Oriented Training Course
comments.content:
Good evening, JMG, and thank you for this ongoing opportunity.

In a previous Magic Monday (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/68693.html) you outlined this magical training course featuring the AODA system:


“You need to start by building a really solid foundation. That means daily practice of the SoP, meditation, and divination, with an emphasis on meditation -- 30 minutes a day of actual meditation should be your minimum for the first two years, working up to an hour a day thereafter. Once you've got the basic method of the SoP down cold, the expansions given in my book The Druid Magic Handbook are your next step, and the steps after that are in a forthcoming book of mine, The Dolmen Arch. Meanwhile get a solid background in natural magic -- the magical properties of herbs, stones, etc. -- and do a lot of practical work with these, and master at least two systems of divination inside and out, so your readings are precise and stone cold accurate. Once you've done that -- not before -- get some good books on spagyrics (herbal alchemy) and get to work on that; spagyric work is a great way to accelerate your inner development, once you've got the basics of magic down.

That's the practical work, and if you do it with sufficient intensity it'll take you ten years of hard work. You also need to put serious time into study. That means mythology and folklore, the raw material from which magic springs; the history of magic, so you know what your roots are and can make sense of older texts and traditions; the literature of magic itself, from the Graeco-Egyptian magical papyri straight on through the latest postmodern avant-garde stuff; and at least some grounding in philosophy as well. To achieve what you want to achieve, you're going to have to have a really solid intellectual understanding of magic as well as a really solid practical grasp of it; it's going to take you roughly the amount of work you'd expect to put in to earn a B.A., M.A., and Ph.D. at a really good university.”


I’d love to hear how you would modify this approach for someone working with the CGD system. I’m guessing you would still recommend daily ritual (LRP + Exercise of the Central Ray instead of SoP), meditation, and divination while working through the CGD book in tandem with The Mysteries of Merlin (and then the Celtic Cabbalah book once it comes out). I’m also guessing you’d recommend building up a mastery of natural magic and spagyrics during this time, plus mastering divination via both geomancy and the Coelbren. Lastly, I assume your basic outline of magical study still holds as is.

Would you add to, emphasize, or alter anything here?

(By the way, I think that what you’ve done with the Celtic Golden Dawn is really rad. I love the overall structure, aesthetic, and the emphases on alchemy, conjuration, consecration, and transformation. So thank you for all the work you’ve done there and for making it publicly available.)

Thanks!
-Colter

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content: That's correct -- and it's what The Celtic Golden Dawn is meant to lead up to. Another book working with the same system, The Litany of the Tree of Life, will be out later this year, and that's well worth adding into the system. Once you've finished the CGD book, I'd encourage you to read Regardie's The Golden Dawn if you haven't already done so, and if you have, read it again; see what you can adapt to Celtic GD terms from that source. (There's a vast amount in there, and very few modern mages have done more than dabbled with it.)

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content:
Excellent, thanks! I’m looking forward to The Litany of the Tree of Life. I have a couple follow-on questions:

1) I’ve also seen you recommend working with the Watchtower ritual in Regardie’s Ceremonial Magic in conjunction with a traditional Golden Dawn path. I don’t have any experience with that book or ritual — is that something that could be adapted to the CGD path?

2) Do you have any suggestions for a first foray into magical history?

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's certainly something you could do, but I recommend finishing the training in the CGD book first.

2) Choose a period and a country that interests you and see what you can find. Always do some reading in the general and cultural history of that place and time, so you get a sense of how magic fits into the wider context.

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content: What is "The Litany of the Tree of Life" about?

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content: It's a set of prayers and invocations meant to call on the energies of the spheres and paths of the Tree of Life. William Gray had a similar project, the Holy Office of the Tree of Life, which I worked with when I was doing Gray's Sangreal Sodality system; it was keyed to his idiosyncratic version of the Cabala, but I liked the idea and so borrowed it (with credit) for a set of workings for my idiosyncratic versions of the Cabala.

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content: Will this book be in addition to ‘The Celtic Cabala’ book mentioned in the FAQ, or is it the same thing under a different title?

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content: It's a different book.

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content: I turn 62 in a couple of months.  Is there any point in me working on this, since I’ll be 71, God willing, when I finish?

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content: Sure. Anything you don't complete in this life can be picked up again in a future life -- and the benefits of any kind of spiritual practice carry over from life to life. That and the development of personal creative abilities are among the things that don't go away when your current body does.

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content:
Once you've got the basic method of the SoP down cold, the expansions given in my book The Druid Magic Handbook are your next step, and the steps after that are in a forthcoming book of mine, The Dolmen Arch.

Something I've thought of asking for a little while now, are those SoP expansions (three cauldrons, and so on) appropriate to add if one is not planning to do the DMH?

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content: Sure. They're also included in the Dolmen Arch, and can be used generally by people working with the system.

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content:
Not the OP: I was going to post a question about divided will when it comes to pursuing spiritual goals while balancing the rest of life's needs, but rather synchronously this comment popped up.

I have a similar goal to the OP, I'd really like to pursue magical practice and learn all I can from it (but in the DMH/DA path, not CGD). Yet I found magical practice relatively late in life (in my early 40s) and I'm still left in the lifestyle and habits of a middle-class life: I have a salary class job, for example. For years, I have been trying to lower expenses as I know that is something I can control, so that I need less money to live, and I have been freeing up time by reducing the amount of time spent in unproductive pursuits (like TV). I have been fairly successful with this, and I use a great deal of my free time to pursue magical studies.

I realize I have two challenges and a choice to make: how to A) find a way to earn a living beyond retirement age, because I realize for many reasons that being able to retire is going to be impossible, or B) pursue the intense period of study you've outlined above.

1) Do you have any general advice here?
2) Do you think there is some way to combine these goals? I know occultism is so varied, but I wonder if there an effective, ethical, and safe way to combine occultism with earning a living (I know writing is one way, as you've shown).

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: CGD-Oriented Training Course
replies.content:
1) Spend some time figuring out what you want, and then make that your intention and go for it.

2) It's perfectly possible to combine them; that's been the story of my adult life. You have to be ready to tolerate a certain amount of voluntary poverty, but there are many jobs that will pay you enough to live on, and still leave time free to practice magic.

comments.title: A Handful of Short Questions
comments.content:
Hello,

Happy late birthday! I did not realize until after I had posted last week. I've got a few fairly short questions, but if that's being greedy, I can space them out over the next few weeks.

1) I've been practicing the Heathen variants of the LBRP and Middle Pillar exercise you posted here and am finding them very helpful - any updates on your "Heathen Golden Dawn" book with Sven Eriksen?
2) A few technical questions for doing the Heathen LRP:
- In the Sign of the Hammer, should I continue to visualize the beam of light from above when picturing myself as Mjollnir? Should I continue one or both of those visualizations when I vibrate "Wod"?
- When tracing out the pentagrams and circle, should I stand in the center and rotate in place, pointing my arm like a clock hand, or should I go out to the perimeter and walk along it, walking in a circle and pausing at each cardinal direction and facing out? Or something else?
- When vibrating the names of the Gods and Goddesses for each pentagram, should I be visualizing them?
- What pose should I stand in when naming the mythic items (Gugnir, Gjallarhorn, Sumrbrandr, and Draupnir) and the stars and Light of the Aesir?
- When naming the above objects, should I visualize them?
- When doing the Sign of the Hammer after the middle pillar exercise, should I continue to visualize the spheres as I do the Hammer Sign visualizations?
3) In the FAQ, you say not to do any magic more advanced than LBRP, the Middle Pillar, Meditation, and Light Divination when you have children under 3 - is pathworking one of those more advanced works to avoid? From the placement and description in Celtic Golden Dawn, it's not clear to me if it is simply a specialized form of mediation, or something else.
4) Besides daily ritual, meditation, and divination, do you have any recommendations on how to develop occult senses? I'm extremely verbal/analytical and have some trouble turning that off for other modes of thinking/sensing.
5) You've said you've read Bonewits's books - are they helpful at all? Anything in there to watch out for (besides the antipathy to the Druid revival you've discussed before)?
Real Magic was my first introduction to occultism other than my first girlfriend's Wiccan books, and its bibliography became my main guide to what else I should read for a while. I'm wondering if it's worth revisiting or if there's anything I might have picked up from it that I ought to rethink.

Thanks,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: A Handful of Short Questions
replies.content:
1) None, I'm sorry to say.

2) Experiment with all of these and see what works best for you.

3) Pathworking is a specialized form of meditation. You can do it all day without influencing children.

4) You might look at the exercises in W.E. Butler's How to Read the Aura, Practice Psychometry, Telepathy, and Clairvoyance.

5) I find Bonewits' books useless, to be frank, but your mileage may vary. From my perspective they're sales brochures and nothing more.

Glad to hear on pathworking and thank you for the Butler recommendation - I have Apprenticed to Magic and I adapted his exercise on looking for things out in the world related to the sephira you are currently studying and taking notes for learning the runes a while back.

Given your previous comments on Bonewits, I'm not shocked. I guess I'm glad your response wasn't something like "they're full of dangerous nonsense, you'll have to unlearn anything you picked up." If nothing else, I am grateful to Real Magic for giving me a path into taking magic seriously from a skeptical materialist viewpoint. Due to your writings and my own experience, that's not where I am now, but I might not have even entertained a lot of this stuff without a trial run of "this is just a fancy way to mess with your subconscious."

replies.title: Re: A Handful of Short Questions
replies.content:
Even though I started this thread asking for advice, here's what I've found stumbling my way through things after jumping off with Real Magic 5-6 years ago:

  • Real Magic does have a fairly useful/comprehensive bibliography, but I trust Bonewits' annotations there less than I once did. It seems like a pretty good rule of thumb is "If Bonewits and JMG both say something is worthwhile or important, it very likely is"

  • As for criteria to look for, the number one thing I like to see is specific, clearly described exercises. W.E. Butler is fantastic for this (Apprenticed to Magic is the one I know by him). After that, it's a matter of trying to filter for nonsense, which is tough when you might have grown up thinking all occult stuff is nonsense. For me, there seems to be a good correlation between depth of familiarity with "normal" history/philosophy/religion and with not being a total crank. Proper citations and a thorough bibliography that is not only occult writings is a good sign. Open-mindedness to other beliefs and traditions also seems to correlate with reasonable folks, but it's possible for that to be taken a bit too far in my view, as seen in the stereotypical "a little bit of everything" flavor of New Ageism

  • Our good host's own writing has been very helpful for me - in the past few months I've read World Full of Gods and re-visited the "Foundations of Magical Practice" posts and actually started following their advice. For me, the combination of taking the Gods seriously as "actually there" plus daily ritual, divination, and meditation has gotten me a handful of strong religious experiences and spiritual insights in a couple of months, but as always, YMMV

  • JMG's Encyclopedia of the Occult isn't really a "how to", but it's useful for getting your bearings when you see something referenced and you want to understand what tradition it comes from. Likewise, his ongoing Magical History of the USA has been helpful for building up a roadmap of different occult traditions, what kind of folks founded/practiced them, and what kinds of lives they led. That's been useful for deciding things like "Hmmm, maybe Crowley's stuff is not the way I want to go"

  • If you are interested in the religious side of things, it's helpful to find a book that gives an overview of that "scene" and what kinds of different groups there are - again, it might help you in assessing what kind of assumptions and worldview an author might be bringing. My beliefs lean Germanish, and I found Northern Tradition for the Solitary Practitioner, which had a brief overview of this kind, helpful for getting a more solid handle on things I had gotten a vague impression of through other sources, like the tension between stricter historical reconstructionists and more gnosis-focused folks. I don't know if such a book exists for other major threads of polytheism or not. I imagine this is less critical if you plan on following something Judeo-Christian based, like original GD.



Thanks,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: A Handful of Short Questions
replies.content:
Thanks Jeff.

I have the Northern Tradition and know the two authors quite well. I have adopted it to Roman practice. Thanks for the other suggestions.

replies.title: Re: A Handful of Short Questions
replies.content:
My take on Bonewits's books is about the same as our host's: sales brochures for the author and the persona that he flaunted.

The best of the lot was his Real Magic, which was not particularly informative or scholarly. (The others by him that I have read not that good.) His bibliography was helpful. So were the few paragraphs he devoted to the "Conjuring Up Philip" experiments at Toronto, which I had not heard of until then.

FWIIW, "Philip" was a fictitious person from a bygone century. Though he was a fiction, he could communicate with experimenters who were holding spirit seances just as readily as real dead people could. This shed a lot of light for me on what Alan Moore was able to accomplish magically with his invented God named Glykon.

replies.title: Re: Synchronicities
replies.content:
"Bristol" was a single county in the 1600s, part of the old Plymouth Colony. In 1691 Massachusetts annexed Plymouth Colony, but not all of it: Bristol County remained disputed territory for several decades. Eventually the county got divided, roughly half and half, between Massachusetts and Rhode Island. That happened a few decades before the US declared its independence from Britain. Culturally, old "Bristol County" still feels like a single entity, despite lying in two adjacent states.

The history of old New England is very complex indeed, and its various cultural (and magical) regions don't align at all well with the boundaries between the New England states. It's a fascinating place to live and work in.

comments.title: Lovecraft, Contact, and Scattershot Fun
comments.content:
Hello, Mr. Greer, and thank you for another Magic Monday!
This week I have three questions for you.

comments.title: "She who could not breathe out"
comments.content:
Howdy, all.

This question is for JMG and the commentariat.

In Gateways through Light and Shadow, Frater Ashen Chassan and Ben Mac Stoifan ask Anael - the archangel of Venus:

"In Faery lore, what is the meaning or truth behind Lucifer falling through Venus to the Earth Mother?"

Anael replies:

"Some will hear this spoken or hear this teaching, and may see it as naive; as fairies traipsing on dust.

"When Lucifer fell, when he fell through Venus . . . he fell through me. His love for 'she who could not breathe out' was so great that he severed himself utterly from the Father. I do not know his destiny; it is a mystery kept even from me. But when briefly he passed through me, I could feel his longing. The longing of a broken heart . . . for knowledge of the other.

"For an Angel to do this it is *such* pain you humans have no conception. But I suspect that in darkness, there is something inexplicably light. This is all I can say of this for now."


So, I find the whole passage really interesting, but my question for y'all is: who is "she who could not breathe out"? The transcription of Anael's response puts that title in quotes, and I've never heard the reference before. Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: "She who could not breathe out"
replies.content: Hmm! I have no idea. That isn't a term I've ever encountered before.

replies.title: Re: "She who could not breathe out"
replies.content:
Looking at some of the threads I've been following lately, my first thought there would be either the Virgin Mary and/or archetype of which the Theotokos is an aspect. (IIRC there's some Romantic-era thought concerning the Eternal Feminine that might be of interest there, though I've long since lost any references about that.)

(Mind you, it's not out of the question that said archetype *is* the Earth Mother, in which case the question and response has already answered your question for you. That said, if my instincts are right on what that response is referring to, Whoever or whatever "she who could not breathe out" is referring to should be associated with the Moon and AFAIK Earth Mother is not.)

- pretentious_username

replies.title: Re: "She who could not breathe out"
replies.content: I’ll just offer that as I read the passage, before I knew what your question would be, my read on “she who could not breathe out” was the Earth Mother (in contrast to the sky Father who Lucifer severed connection with by falling). The core of the planet, being ultra dense and by far the the largest source of gravitic pull in our environment, in a sense never breathes out but only pulls in. Perhaps there are subtler spiritual levels of this inward “breathing” as well. Anyway, this all of course suggests Lucifer’s kingdom, the underworld.

replies.title: Re: "She who could not breathe out"
replies.content:
I've never heard of that title before reading that passage myself. What sprang to my mind when I read what was maybe something to do with the Holy Spirit in some way or possibly Anael herself. In The Mystical Qabalah, Dion Fortune refers to the Holy Spirit as a feminine force; as I recall she was quoting MacGregor-Mathers in his Qabalah Unveiled. Worthy of meditation I think.

Sincerely,
Brother Josephus

replies.title: Re: "She who could not breathe out"
replies.content:
I love working with Angels and Angels of Venus in particular, so I had a brief channeling with Anael to see if I could answer your question. I'm paraphrasing, because she communicated using visions and impressions appearing from a flame.

The answer surprised me, but makes total sense: 'she who could not breathe out' is Lillith, the Qlipoth of Malkuth. She lays down for no one and so Lucifer fell for her. In a sense, his love is an unconditional love of incarnation. Lucifer has a reputation for evil because the love of the incarnations of goodness is common, but to love all other incarnations equally is unique to him among angels. I was also told that hell is not a permanent fixture of this reality but a teaching tool. It will be gone (at least to us), once humanity evolves beyond the need for it. I can't explain further because the example she used was very personal, but I feel the truth of this deeply.

For further study, I recommend reading and rereading JMG's Paths of Wisdom, because even though the reference to Lillith is brief, you have to learn Malkuth to understand Lillith and you have to learn the entire tree of life to understand Malkuth. It's also best to learn about demonic entities in a roundabout way, if you can manage it, so as to keep your energy attuned to the angelic.

Speaking of which, Anael also said she loves you, AlexanderMarcus, and wants a deeper relationship with you.

replies.title: Re: "She who could not breathe out"
replies.content:
A Myth of Lucifer’s Fall concerning Love for All Incarnation

At the beginning, there was God or Everything; all meaning, all light, all but Nothingness. That was the “Dark” region yet unexplored. A most magnificent, beautiful and loving part of Everything who carried seeds of Everything within himself, Lucifer, agreed to fall into the Darkness – Nothingness; as he was falling, he was also falling apart, diminishing, spending himself in the fall, he became a being scattered in an infinite, countless points – nothings enriched by the tiniest passing seeds of Everything he had yet been bearing before reaching the other side…Nothingness.
To be searched for by every of those parts as their possibility to find the Original Whole; to be searched for through different dimensions of space, time, meaning…the search is almost the complete opposite of the Fall: recovering and healing Lucifer – but this time freely and forever growing. With every part gaining a little more freedom, meaning, health, wholeness, the angel would grow and heal and bring more glory than could have ever been done without the Fall: for every zero point would gain a seed of God through the Fall of the Angel, who fell for Love and out of Love to the deepest abyss there was – to Nothingness ("She who could not breathe out") and then for ever rising back.

Markéta

replies.content: In your opinion JMG, is there evidence that Hu Gadarn, and other revival Druid deities, were worshipped by Welsh Celtic cultures at some point? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It depends on the deity. Belinus and Cernunnos, for example, were certainly worshipped in ancient times. Hu Gadarn? Almost certainly not. One of the great lessons of religious history, however, is that gods come and go, and a newly risen god can be well worth invoking. Consider Odin, for example -- all the evidence I've seen suggests that he was quite a recent arrival in the Germanic pantheon, and Tyr (whose name is cognate with the names of other Indo-European high gods such as Zeus) was the chief god of the Germanic tribes before his arrival. Odin is still a very powerful god and worthy of reverence.

replies.title: Menstrual cycles
replies.content:
Back in the 70s when feminists were looking to escape dependence on the patriarchal medical system there were discussions of this sort of thing. If I recall correctly one of the recommendations was to literally synch one's cycles to the moon by avoiding artificial light after nightfall and especially in one's bedroom and if possible sleeping with curtains open and exposed to the moon. The design was to link ovulation to the full moon and menstruation to the dark. Obviously difficult in the city if the traffic light and the neon from the bar next door is coming through your bedroom window, or even the safety lights in the parking lot of suburban apt. I know there was a book advocating this as a natural method of birth control, but don't recall the title--you might be able to locate it under that subject and narrowing the publication date to 1965-80. For heaven's sake don't believe any claims of efficacy as birth control. Such 'natural' methods may suffice to space pregnancies out, but not to prevent them. There was a lot of inaccurate information about "native" practices, probably because sensible women aren't going to blab that stuff even to their own men, let alone some foreign man with a notebook and inscrutable reasons for asking.

One thing that was forgotten in some of this search for natural menstruation is that in a non-industrial culture women didn't menstruate as frequently as women in modern societies. If you marry shortly after your periods start and have no effective means of birth control you will spend much of your life either pregnant or breastfeeding (which suppresses menstruation). So native women were not trotting off to the red tent every dark moon to hang out, unless they were celibate or infertile.

Rita

comments.title: Before the Butlerian Jihad
comments.content:
Sometime this past week, somebody shared this link in one of your blogs, a Kickstarter project to fund an “AI powered” “gamified micro-learning” memory training app with virtual 3D “premade memory palaces”. It seems to having been a knockdown success of a Kickstarter, blowing way past their goal of $10,000 to rake in $387,000 so far with a few weeks left to go.

First of all, I hope you’ve been developing the memory component in your upcoming modular Occult Training system, because there’s clearly a big market for it. Then again, it seems a lot of people prefer their mental skills hooked to a paid subscription app...

I found this part amusing:

Why not learn mnemonics from a textbook?

It is certainly possible to develop memorization skill from specialized textbooks. Besides the long hours of regular training, this approach has several limitations. A major one is having to construct a large enough, usable mind palace out of well-known locations that are themselves limited.


This seems to me something that would only worry someone who had never actually attempted to build a memory palace before. Then again, I suppose that’s the target audience.

Anyway, my actual question is about the following:

The name of our game component technology is Mind Palace Virtual Implant and refers to the virtual transfer of pre-made memory palaces to a person's long-term memory. These structured environments will later be recreated by a person's imagination as a tulpa and used as storage space to help encode, store, and retrieve necessary information once needed.


Hmmm. Implanting a tulpa. What could possibly go wrong?

Now, I’m assuming that the AI aspect is mainly hype, and there are no inhuman minds controlling these “tulpas”. And further, I’m assuming that the writers of this sales copy don’t actually know what a tulpa is, and are just applying the word because it’s been trendy on the internet lately.

Still. Let’s assume the worst for a moment; that in the spirit of all of the tales of devil’s bargains which give exactly what they advertise, there really is some sort of disembodied personality seeking to implant itself in people’s minds by way of gamified AI memory palace-based learning. Could you see any mechanism for this to actually occur? Whether through a video game which seeks to get the user to memorize every part of it, or via some other method?

replies.title: Re: Before the Butlerian Jihad

comments.title: Divided Will and interests
comments.content:
I’ve been reading up on what you wrote about will, will as opposed to desire and your series on will training.

I realised that I, perhaps in common with many people in this age, have a widely divided will, not just a bifurcation but really a wild branching.

I enjoy reading in a wide variety of fields from fiction to history to languages to the latest economic trends, I work out, I train in wrestling and grappling, I’m interested in occultism and have done a daily practice for a good number of years.

However, it seems that I end up being a dabbler in all of these fields rather than pushing my personal boundaries after a while.

To use a lifting analogy, I guess I topped out at double body weight squats, which puts me well above average but nowhere near the elite.

I’ve been meditating and journaling on this topic for a while. I realise that determining one’s priority is really up to one’s own values and interests.

I’ve seen many people clearly devote themselves to one field whether it’s wealth, martial arts, or spirituality and make huge trade offs in other aspects of their life.

For example, for every professional mixed martial artist making money off fights, there are a hundred others who are barely making ends meet on dead end jobs while training hard; even among professional fighters, I’ve heard that many, except those who get big enough to do sponsorship deals, still struggle financially. Other people devote their will to wealth accumulation but neglect their health and development of their minds, and so on.

I feel like I want a middle path. I want to increase my income and be financially comfortable, but I don’t care about running a huge business or “changing the world” in the Silicon Valley startup sense. I’d also like to keep progressing along my own spiritual path, and be fighting fit.

Do you think it’s possible to get anywhere like that? Or is significant progress only possible with sole devotion to one undivided will?

My guess is that at the very least one would need to have one predominant will at a particular stage of life? So for example, if I’m concentrating on increasing my income for a period of time, I can very well keep doing my practices, my training and so on, but if a conflict comes between income-increase and other goals, I choose income-increase?

replies.title: Re: Divided Will and interests
replies.content:
Of course you can live a successful life with many interests. Most successful people do just that. You probably won't be one of those world-changing successes, but you can do well. I'll use myself as an example; I set out to become a successful writer, to have a happy marriage, to pursue magic systematically, and to stay healthy into advanced old age. I haven't reached advanced old age yet, but other than that, I've succeeded quite capably in my goals.

There are two basic skills that are necessary in a middle path of the sort you've sketched out. The first is to work out an effective compromise among the things you want to do -- this much time for your spiritual life, this much time for your fitness and martial arts, and the rest for making money and for the ordinary requirements of life. The second is to reject anything that doesn't further at least one of your goals. Does it make you wealthy, or support your spiritual path, or help you stay fighting fit, or meet some basic biological need? No? Chuck it out. You can get very, very far with that kind of approach.

comments.title: Physiognomy
comments.content:
Speaking of memory practice, as my job currently has me meeting many new groups of people on a regular basis, I’ve been experimenting with using simple memory techniques to learn names quickly. I do use facial or body features as memorable items to link with pun connections to names when these features are notable, but I find so far that clothing and hairstyle cues work for me better in general right now. The weakness here is that usually when I meet anyone a second or third time, they are no longer wearing the same clothes or even hairstyle. But if I try to remember simply by face, I don’t generally get enough of a strong peg on which to hang my mnemonic.

It occurred to me that the study of physiognomy, in the older occult sense of something similar to phrenology or palm reading, might give me better tools to work with— as presumably there are systematic, codified ways of breaking down the features of any face into particular parts, each with their own meanings and symbolism. If so, even if it turned out to be worthless as a predictive art (though I’m willing to give it its fair shot), it seems to me that it could be much more useful as an instant, ready made mnemonic peg. E.g. if a girl named Momoka (which I’d already be instantly transforming to a possible pair of kanji for the name, “peach flower”) had, I don’t know, I’ll make up some Physiognomy terminology here, an oblique browline and a Sagittarian cleft chin, I could put a centaur with a flower bow in her cleft, shooting peaches in diagonal lines out from her eyebrows. Or if I knew that the shape of her nose signified compassion I could imagine her gently passing out flowering peaches, gripping them with her nose as she does so like an elephant would. Or somesuch.

Anyway, as I’ve never seen you mention the subject of physiognomy I don’t imagine you’ve studied it, but may I ask— do you happen to know if there are any sources on the ancient occult practice of physiognomy which have particular renown, particularly in English? Or otherwise know where one might learn more about it?

comments.title: Involution, evolution and progress?
comments.content:
Hi JMG and commentariat
I was wondering how to raise this new article in Magic Monday. Its a neat synchronicity that it also uses the word "involution", though in a different sense to the illustration for this weeks Magic Monday. I think it raises other themes you will find familiar: https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-china-57328508
I find this story facinating for a couple of reasons. First - Young Chinese people seem to be re-discovering attitudes broadly similar to philosophical Taoism formulated in China about 2500 years ago. replies.title: Re: Involution, evolution and progress?
replies.content:
1) Every culture has a certain number of possible options for dealing with its own innate problems. Right now the Chinese government is behaving just like every other entrenched Chinese government since the time of Qin Shi Huangdi, pursuing policies that further the collective good at the expense of the individual, and so young people are responding like every other Chinese generation since even before Huangdi's time, and embracing the Taoist alternative. That'll keep on happening as long as Chinese civilization endures.

2) It's very fashionable to confuse progress with evolution. Biological evolution is adaptation to changing circumstances, full stop, end of sentence. Spiritual evolution is the unfolding (this is what the word "evolution" literally means) of potentials that were taken on in a latent form during involution. In either case, linear improvement going on forever -- the standard current fantasy of progress -- isn't an option.

If the vaccine is the problem, she's facing unknown health consequences for an unknown time; therefore, to reduce her stress, she convinces herself that the vaccine can't possibly be the problem. If anyone else refuses the vaccine, that makes it harder for her to keep telling herself that the vaccine can't possibly be the problem, and so they have to be vilified and made to take the vaccine, otherwise she can't keep telling herself the vaccine is fine. The more of a bully she becomes, furthermore, the more entrenched she becomes in that attitude, since otherwise she has to admit that she's been behaving like a bully. It really is a difficult trap to escape.

comments.title: Comments on Churches

The sacraments are important to my occult practice. By consuming my God in the Eucharist, I feel a current of divine power for magical work. Confession places me in greater contact with this divine power, too. The bread and butter of my magical practice is creating sacramentals and devotions to accomplish my goals.

Finally, because of the Catholic distinction between latria and dulia, I feel free to develop relationships with other Deities, too. These have been very beneficial to me.

I should probably address the "like and dislike" part, too. As far as the Lutheran and Episcopal churches that I once attended: they were very modernist, very liberal. Liberal Christian theology is not something that interests me. It is largely devoid of the supernatural and places a lot of emphasis on "social justice warrior" ideology with some spirituality thrown in. That's not my cup of tea. Their sacraments do seem to have some kind of effect but it is rather subtle. Their openness to other religions is something to like.

On the Pentecostals and the Mormons: I liked how both could really place you in touch with the Holy Spirit or Holy Ghost. I'm pretty sure that with some serious commitment to the practices of each of these churches, one can develop a strong relationship with the Spirit and receive the benefits of that. While attending Mormon services, I actually had a vision of a scene from the Book of Mormon. As far as the Pentecostals, I'm actually in the process of finding a Charismatic Catholic prayer group so that I can see how well it meshes with sacramental practices.

Finally, on the subject of Catholicism: I dislike the new rites for the sacraments. These "Novus Ordo" rites were put together after the Second Vatican Council. They robbed the sacraments of a good deal of their magical power, though they are not totally useless. Whenever possible, I prefer to receive the sacraments according to the traditional forms. As for what I like about Catholicism, well, the list would probably be endless. I do believe in the doctrines of the Church, though my interpretations might sometimes push the limits of orthodoxy.

replies.title: Re: Comments on Churches
replies.content:
I grew up Anabaptist (very conservative Mennonite), have attended Presbyterian, Episcopal, and Catholic churches, and am now Catholic.

Anabaptist paths emphasize doing and community, and are very uneasy environments in which to be "different"; their rituals are remarkably powerful (their promises genuinely bind them in my experience), but are only for those who are whole-heartedly committed to the church. (The more assimilated groups may be different.)

Presbyterian (Reformed) paths emphasize thinking carefully and knowledge. They have a real commitment to their faith, but it's not very experiential or sacramental, and tends to draw bright lines where shades of gray would be more helpful.

Episcopal churches can have great community, and I still miss my old church--but it's so very Establishment-aligned at this point that the politics seem to ahve largely swamped the religion. That said--if you can find an Episcopal church that has good music and a devout priest, the services can be quite powerful--and extremely tolerant of religious dissent.

Catholicism in theory is great; in practice it varies a lot. Confession is amazingly helpful; the Rosary is a great tool for prayer and meditation; the Mass done well can be a great source of strength--but I'd say the English Mass is only done well 10% of the time. I'd say try several parishes before deciding "this doesn't seem to be the place for me"--there's a huge amount of variation.

SamChevre

I went to a Presbyterian church until I was a teenager. I stopped going when it occurred to me that nobody there really believed in it - i.e. I didn't see anybody actually putting the teachings into practice. In retrospect, I think that most of those folks were earnestly trying to live good lives, and I was probably overzealous in expecting them all to share my youthful idealism. replies.title: Re: Comments on Churches
replies.content:
I went to Presbyterian and Catholic churches as a kid. I'm now Orthodox.

Presbyterians: I liked the generally respectful attitude of the worship services, and the fact that most folks there seemed to be really trying to do right and be good people. It wasn't enough to hold me in the long run. What I didn't like was that, like many Protestant churches, it rigidly self-segregates by socioeconomic class: Presbyterians are overwhelmingly a professional-class church. Also, its theology, and its version of salvation, seem to be largely exercises in intellectual assent. This is great if you want to believe *that there is a God,* but you never want to be unsettled by His actual presence. One feels they try to serve God, but also keep Him at a safe distance... and those goals seem incompatible.

Catholics: I liked the respectful liturgy, and especially the things chanted or recited as a congregation: one really felt there was something *there*. But I watched all of this get phased out in favor of guitar masses with hymns set to 60s pop tunes, over the course of my childhood. I like that they do *not* seem to self-segregate by socioeconomic class: you can be dirt poor, and still be a fully accepted and participating member of a Catholic parish (at my old Presbyterian church, they'd assume you were only there to avail yourself of the food pantry). But most of the parishes I have been to beyond about 1990 seem quite dead and devoid of... God. There are some exceptions: the missal-publishing company that ruined Catholic liturgical music in the US did not completely infiltrate non-English speaking immigrant parishes, or Eastern Rite Catholic churches. Those parishes were likewise not as affected by other various "modernization" schemes, and some of them still seem to have some spiritual heft. But it can be hard to integrate into such a parish. The one I often attended, growing up, was an immigrant parish and they still have not adopted "modern" church music, even though they use a lot more English now-- and the archdiocese leaves them alone. For a long time, they maintained a robust tradition of group chanting that was uplifting and inspiring, even though I only understood one word in twenty. You can also find some "heft" at the services other than Sunday Mass, that most people don't attend: early-morning daily mass, late-night rosaries... that's when the really devoted worshippers show up, and it's a very different experience. One thing that really turns me off about the RC church is hearing so many people talk about the awful experience of lousy catechesis: of joining the church because they feel it's the right thing to do, but basically having to ignore everything they learned in the converts' class: enduring it just to "get your ticket punched". That seems really off.

Eastern Orthodox: That's where I am now, so clearly there's a lot I like, but it can pretty much all be summed up as: "God was there". After that, not much else matters (shrugs). That said, I like the respectful, ancient, liturgical forms. I like the aesthetic: they're one of the few churches that still thinks worship should be beautiful, for all we don't always live up to the ideal. I like that the actual sacraments and disciplines of the church are actually helpful in putting you in touch with the divine. But a lot of them aren't mandatory: it's more like "here's a toolkit of stuff that we know from experience works. If you use the tools you'll get good results. If you don't use the tools, you won't get the results, but we won't kick you out because maybe you'll get to it later." In practice, this is very tolerant and inclusive: everybody's on a different place in their spiritual path, and you need to mind your own progress and not everyone else's. I don't like: ethnophyletism. It's a big problem, unfortunately, and you'll encounter it in way too many parishes, particularly the Greek ones (but it's not just them). I say that with great sadness, as a white-bread American convert member of a Greek parish. There are way too many people who put the "Greek" part before the "Orthodox" part, and if you're not part of the founding ethnic group, it can be really hard to be part of parish life. We have managed it, but only by becoming burrs in the wool of our fellow parishioners: they can't get rid of us, so they've had to get used to us. And now we've been there long enough, they even like us ;) For someone just exploring Orthodoxy, I'd strongly recommend checking out *all* the Orthodox churches in your area, starting with the Antiochian and OCA parishes, which tend to be a lot more welcoming to visitors and converts. The OCA is what happened when the original Russian mission churches in the US lost contact with their patriarch after the Bolshevik Revolution. They're American now: liturgy's in English (but with the nice Russian tunes). The Antiochian churches were founded by immigrants from Syria and Lebanon, mostly. Most of their parishes made a conscious decision to switch to English, in order to not lose their kids as they became Americans. It was a good decision, and they're doing better than most other jurisdictions on retaining cradle members, as well as bringing in converts.

First, thank you for stating that there are no wrong answers in meditation in your introductory post on Levi. Gee, I think I figure something out, I read some more, and realize how much I don't know.

I have ordered Paths of Wisdom and will do my best to read it before asking even more questions, but what I read today just about short-circuited my brain.

I started reading up on Kether. In the metaphor I had built, I had the Solar Logos in the middle of the Merkabah, sending his light in whatever direction he wanted. But then I read that Kether receives "from the Serifot of Malkuth of the domain above it". So when I thought that Gaia, our Spirit Below, was not in the Merkabah proper, I was not correct... the Merkabahs of different domains (planes? sub-planes? I'll get to that in my reading eventually) stack on each other, which means my Spirit Above is another swarm's Spirit Below.

But does that mean that *I'm* (or my swarm? or my swarm's Group Soul?) is the Spirit Below of our Spirit Above?

And then does that mean (here's where my brain short-circuited) that we (our swarm's Group Soul or whatever) is Gaia's Spirit Above?

I know we are to attune to Gaia. I know we are supposed to help her learn. But, jiminy, is my understanding way off? (And if not, why Kether warrants humility makes perfect sense.)

And if I'm on the right track, is me just trying to do my best to understand and follow the Path good enough? My mind is just boggled right now.

Orange Hibernating Centaur

replies.title: Re: Levi and Cabala
replies.content:
Excellent. Keep meditating on this!

It's a standard element of occult philosophy, btw, that the planetary spirit of Earth (and all other planets) evolves by sharing in the evolution of the beings who live upon her, and so yes, our Malkuth is her Kether. Equally, our Kether is the Malkuth of the Solar Logos.

Mr. Greer,

replies.title: Re: Initiation of the Nadir
replies.content: The problem is that the moment the initiation of the Nadir comes up people start larding it with a thick layer of wish fulfillment and self-glorifying fantasy. The crucial thing to keep in mind is that it's not a moment -- it's a prolonged process that will unfold over centuries.

replies.title: Re: Initiation of the Nadir

If you think of occasions when you've been at the beach as the incoming tide turns and begins receding, it's by no means obvious; for a while, each wave seems to roll up just as far as the ones before, and some may even go a little further here and there; but after a while you notice that there's wet sand above the highest reach of the waves, and bit by bit the water recedes.

comments.title: Muslim background
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Ive been following your page, books, and am a part of your book club for some time now. For that I would like to start by saying thank you for giving time for all of us curious souls out there.

I want to give some background first before my question. I come from a Muslim background that has a very strong belief in magic and the world of spirits. From a young age, I moved away from institutionalized religions into complete dismissal of spirituality. In the past 5 years, I found that I couldnt "dismiss" my strong belief into the world of djinn and magic (Ive had too many encounters throughout life that would prove to me its strong existence in my life). This, transformed my current stance into an agnostic belief that I am trying to make sense of. Given that, I have been for the past recent months introduced to the teachings of qaballah, hermeticisim, and the esoteric beliefs, which I am deeply interested in (also given that I have past experience with Shams Elmaaref and the book of Zohar).

My questions is as follows, given that I can "quickly" jump into a state of meditation which I still remember how to do from my prayers back in the day, do you believe I can meditate using Quran verses?

Also, rather generally, do you have any recommendations for me on how to rightly approach this path?

Thanks alot

replies.title: Re: Muslim background
replies.content:
Of course you can meditate using verses from the Quran. I don't know a lot about Muslim spirituality but I'd be astounded if there wasn't a rich tradition of doing exactly that.

I was taught a way of meditative reading of the Bible that you might find worth trying. The rules are these. Begin with the most important section -- in the Bible, for a Christian, that's one of the Gospels; I don't happen to know enough about the Quran to know if there's a surah that's considered more important or more sacred than the others, but if there is, that's the one to start with.

Practice every day, preferably first thing in the morning. Always begin with a prayer; read a certain amount -- in the Bible, one chapter is a good amount; then meditate on the ideas in that chapter; and finish with a prayer.

The old woman who taught me this was a devout Christian who had been practicing this for I don't know how many decades, and she was one of the most spiritual people I've ever known. She was the kind of person who could walk into a room and it was like someone turned on the lights.

replies.title: Re: Hello

3) I don't have any experience with that method, so I'll simply suggest that you do them at different times of the day and see how things proceed.

comments.title: Jordan Peterson
comments.content:
Recently I've been listening to some of Jordan Peterson's lectures and podcasts and I had this thought, that I thought might be interesting to you JMG, and or the commentariat.

(And I doubt this is the first time someone's noticed this)

I remember you (JMG) writing or saying something to the effect of: if when people encounter a specific person, and the vast majority of them seem to have reactions with high emotion that are inconsistent with logic or normal reactions to things, it's likely theres an archetype at play here.

I think he (JP) is embodying many variations of the archetype of the "father" (though I may be wrong as I know very little about Jung's work)

Here's Jordan Peterson, who as far as I can tell is a genuinely caring human being who really cares about helping people. I think any reasonable person who listens directly to him for more than 30 minutes will come away with that feeling.

And he has helped many people, and often in the comments of videos many people joking call him "dad" or say he is a father figure to them. I think often people who are helped by his work are projecting or seeing the archetype of the positive father, or perhaps teacher or mentor, in JP.

And often the people who vilify him, seem to be projecting the archetype of the Tyrannical Father upon him. And almost seem completely unable to hear the actual arguments he is making, instead misinterpreting them, and attacking what they claim to be his argument. (Strawmanning) Such as in the infamous Cathy Newman interview. Seeing JP as aggressive, or overly controlling, or justifying discrimination.

I also wonder at how his position in the public sphere, and also his philosophical talk, and likely genuine belief and f attempt to embody the Judeo-Christian concept of sacrificing the self, even if it's just some aspects of the self, for the good of the world, how that may be playing out in his life with his hugely positive impact, but also grave illness and intensity of joy and sorrow that he talks about his life having.

Does anyone else have thoughts on this?

It seems to be such a direct case study in archetypes at work in our society that I wanted to point it out!

Thanks JMG and all,

-WindMan

(If this is appropriate for magic Monday, if not and this doesn't go through I can save it for an open post)

replies.title: Re: Jordan Peterson
replies.content: It's a great example. The positive side of the Father archetype is the Wise Old Man, the negative side is the Tyrant, and people project one or the other on them depending on how well they've integrated that part of their own psyches.

replies.title: Re: Dealing with alchohol
replies.content: If you have a bad relationship with alcohol, there'll be other things in your life that are making that bad relationship happen. The mage's way to deal with that is to figure out what's actually going on in your emotional and psychological life that makes you turn to alcohol as an escape, and change those things.

comments.title: Perfection and nothingness
comments.content:
Hi JMG and Everyone,

A couple of weeks ago, lying in bed, awake in the middle of the night, a time when most of my "profound thoughts"seem to come, I was thinking about the concept of perfection and it's relation to the Tree of Life.

A couple of thoughts popped into my mind, that as we (everything with a soul?) ascend, towards a state of perfection we ultimately disappear into the nothingness of Ain. After all what could transcend perfection?

The other thought, that from the "nothingness" of Ain came the "thought" of perfection, which began the process of manifestation.

I've only a very limited knowledge of the T.O.L. but thoughts anyone?

May also request again as I did on the other blog, for prayers for my dear little dog Milo and I as we grieve the loss, last Wednesday, of our beautiful girl, "Molly Perfect - nearly" as I used to call her.
She was actually the inspiration for those late night thoughts as mentioned above, lying on the bed with me, as she did every night for over 13 years.
Now she rest in my back yard with self sown flowers replanted on her little grave.

Poor Milo is quite bewildered about where his walking and sniffing partner has gone, so maybe an extra special one for him!

Blessings to all, and may I suggest you go and hug someone you love - 2 or 4 legged, and let them know how special they are. You won't run out of love if you use it :-)

Helen in Oz

replies.title: Re: Perfection and nothingness
replies.content:
Good energy on the way for Milo!

As for perfection, well, as I see it perfection isn't a single thing. A perfect teacup can be transcended by a perfect potter...
